title: Ensure hardware MFA is enabled for the 'root' user account

input_row.title: Ensure hardware MFA is enabled for the 'root' user account
input_row.description: The 'root' user account is the most privileged user in an AWS account. MFA adds an extra layer of protection on top of a user name and password. With MFA enabled, when a user signs in to an AWS website, they will be prompted for their user name and password as well as for an authentication code from their AWS MFA device. For Level 2, it is recommended that the 'root' user account be protected with a hardware MFA.
input_row.rationale: A hardware MFA has a smaller attack surface than a virtual MFA. For example, a hardware MFA does not suffer the attack surface introduced by the mobile smartphone on which a virtual MFA resides. Note: Using hardware MFA for numerous AWS accounts may create a logistical device management issue. If this is the case, consider implementing this Level 2 recommendation selectively for the highest security AWS accounts, while applying the Level 1 recommendation to the remaining accounts.
input_row.audit: Perform the following to determine if the 'root' user account has a hardware MFA setup: 1. Run the following command to determine if the 'root' account has MFA setup: aws iam get-account-summary | grep "AccountMFAEnabled" The AccountMFAEnabled property is set to 1 will ensure that the 'root' user account has MFA (Virtual or Hardware) Enabled. If AccountMFAEnabled property is set to 0 the account is not compliant with this recommendation. 2. If AccountMFAEnabled property is set to 1, determine 'root' account has Hardware MFA enabled. Run the following command to list all virtual MFA devices: Internal Only - General aws iam list-virtual-mfa-devices If the output contains one MFA with the following Serial Number, it means the MFA is virtual, not hardware and the account is not compliant with this recommendation: "SerialNumber": "arn:aws:iam::_<aws_account_number>_:mfa/root-account-mfa-device"
input_row.remediation: Note: To manage MFA devices for the AWS 'root' user account, you must use your 'root' account credentials to sign in to AWS. You cannot manage MFA devices for the 'root' account using other credentials. Perform the following to establish a hardware MFA for the 'root' user account: 1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the IAM console at https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/. 2. Choose Dashboard, and under Security Status, expand Activate MFA on your root account. 3. Choose Activate MFA. 4. In the wizard, choose A hardware MFA device and then choose Next Step. 5. In the Serial Number box, enter the serial number that is found on the back of the MFA device. 6. In the Authentication Code 1 box, enter the six-digit number displayed by the MFA device. You might need to press the button on the front of the device to display the number. 7. Wait 30 seconds while the device refreshes the code, and then enter the next six-digit number into the Authentication Code 2 box. You might need to press the button on the front of the device again to display the second number. 8. Choose Next Step. The MFA device is now associated with the AWS account. The next time you use your AWS account credentials to sign in, you must type a code from the hardware MFA device. Remediation for this recommendation is not available through AWS CLI.
input_row.additional_information: IAM User account 'root' for us-gov cloud regions does not have console access. This control is not applicable for us-gov cloud regions. Internal Only - General

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Sign in to the AWS Management Console and open the IAM console at: https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/.
2. Select “Dashboard” from the IAM navigation pane.
3. Look for the “Security Status” section and check if “Activate MFA on your root account” is listed. If it is not listed, it implies that MFA is not configured.
4. If it is listed, click “Activate MFA” to expand the section. 
5. Once expanded, confirm whether MFA is activated. If it is, you will need to determine if it is hardware or virtual.
6. To verify if a hardware MFA is set up, run the following command in the AWS CLI: 
   ```bash
   aws iam get-account-summary | grep "AccountMFAEnabled"
   ```
7. If this command returns `AccountMFAEnabled: 1`, it indicates that MFA is enabled. The next step is to check the type of MFA.
8. Run the following command to list the virtual MFA devices:
   ```bash
   aws iam list-virtual-mfa-devices
   ```
9. Inspect the output for any MFA devices. If an MFA device has a Serial Number in the format `arn:aws:iam::_<aws_account_number>_:mfa/root-account-mfa-device`, it indicates that a virtual MFA is in use rather than a hardware MFA.
10. Verify that no virtual MFA devices are attached to the root account, ensuring compliance with Level 2 requirement for hardware MFA.
11. If compliance is confirmed, document the findings; if not, plan the remediation steps. 

**Expected secure configuration:** A hardware MFA device should be assigned to the 'root' user account with no virtual MFA devices associated.

**How to confirm compliance:** Ensure no virtual MFA devices appear in the output of the list command, and verify that a hardware MFA device is assigned and operational for the root account.
